steps.text: The NRC Will Coordinate with Other Federal Agencies.
Heads of executive departments and agencies submit to the Director of OMB a detailed plan of actions to implement the policies and directives stated in Executive Order 13175.
Recognize important Tribal priorities.
Plan to hold meetings in locations that do not impose significant financial burden on Tribal budgets, when possible.
Seek out group views and the opinions of individuals.

preconditions.text: When planning where to hold meetings with Tribal representatives

constraints.text: Do not consider interactions with Tribal government officials only from your own cultural perspective.
Be sensitive to the cultural perspectives of all persons with whom you interrelate.
Recognize that Tribal governments face the same budgetary constraints as the Federal Government.

steps.text: Plan for the time needed to consult to resolve adverse effects to historic properties at the beginning of the project planning process.
Meet with consulting parties to attempt to reach an agreement on how to resolve adverse effects.
Prepare an agreement document (typically a Memorandum of Agreement [MOA] or Programmatic Agreement [PA]) establishing agreed upon measures.
Request and consider ACHP comments.
Implement all agreed upon measures established in the resulting MOA or PA.

preconditions.text: The proposed undertaking poses a threat of adverse effects on historic properties
The signatories cannot come to an agreement

steps.text: Ensure that properties of religious importance (e.g., traditional cultural properties) are considered during the NHPA Section 106 review process.
Coordinate consideration of properties of religious importance with AIRFA responsibilities.
Coordinate consultation with NHPA Section 106 consultation.

constraints.text: Consultation applies to Federally recognized Indian Tribes, Native Hawaiians, and Alaska Natives.

steps.text: Seek the views of the appropriate SHPO when identifying historic properties, evaluating eligibility, and assessing effects of an undertaking.
Consult with SHPOs when developing MOAs and PAs under NHPA Section 106.
Assume functions of an SHPO with respect to Tribal lands.

preconditions.text: The Secretary of the Interior determines that the Tribal preservation program and the Tribe’s plan describes how the Tribal historic preservation official will carry out the functions.

steps.text: Identify the Tribal contacts whom the staff has established relationships and other potential contacts and their roles and positions within the Tribal communities.
Discuss with the Tribal liaison whether the Tribal government considers the meeting privileged or confidential and thus nonpublic.
Identify the issues and topics for discussion and review, evaluate the agency information to be presented, and determine whether the Tribal meeting is a high level or staff level meeting.
Identify the desired outcomes for the meeting.
Clarify with the Tribal government whether the planned government to government meeting is public or nonpublic.
Identify meeting participants who could include NRC staff, Tribal technical representatives, the NRC senior staff, or the Tribal head or Tribal Council.
Identify the appropriate rank of the NRC representative given the activity involved, the level of interaction, and the established agenda.

constraints.text: Without the Tribal government’s written designation that an upcoming government-to-government meeting is considered privileged or confidential, the NRC staff should document the meeting as publicly available.

steps.text: Contact the Office of the General Counsel for advice on how to respond if a Tribal representative makes a statement pertaining to or asserting Tribal treaty rights.
Discuss with the Tribal liaison whether the Tribal government considers the meeting, including correspondence, the meeting summary, and presentation materials, to be privileged information.
Select meeting sites or locations that are accessible to all parties from both a logistic and economic perspective.

preconditions.text: A Tribal representative makes a statement pertaining to or asserting Tribal treaty rights.

constraints.text: A formal government to government meeting between the NRC and a Tribal government is not a public meeting and should not be made public by the NRC staff.
Remain flexible if leaders decide to revise the meeting agenda.

steps.text: Participate in cross cultural awareness training, look for personal bias, establish professional relationships, and remain open to learning about Tribal customs.
Contact the Office of Nuclear Material Safety and Safeguards (NMSS) Federal, State, and Tribal Liaison Branch for assistance in arranging for translators.
Follow the same protocols in official meetings with Tribal leaders as with other Government leaders.
Address Tribal leaders by their official titles and names.
Introduce Tribal leaders before other participants and invite them to address the meeting first.
Display respect for the Tribe's customs and beliefs during any opening prayer, song, or invocation.
Provide time for people to speak without interruption.

preconditions.text: If Tribal members, especially Tribal elders, appreciate the option of having an interpreter present at the meetings
If a Tribal representative, elder, or spiritual leader opens a meeting with a prayer, a song, or an invocation

constraints.text: Tribal members place great value on listening, and comments are held until all Tribal members have had an opportunity to speak.
Tribal elders, in particular, are afforded great respect when speaking.

steps.text: COMMUNICATE with the Tribe because it is the key to successful relationships.
FAMILIARIZE YOURSELF with the Tribe’s specific information and understand the history and current issues, concerns, and expertise of the Tribe or Tribes with whom you may work.
HOLD FACE-TO-FACE MEETINGS to get to know the Tribe and its technical resource staff and council representatives.
ESTABLISH FAMILIARITY AND CONTINUITY because they are essential elements for establishing and maintaining effective relationships.
KEEP THE FOCUS WHERE IT BELONGS because Tribes and their concerns should be highlighted during introductions and the meeting table.
BE ADAPTABLE by exhibiting flexibility, sensitivity, and empathy in Tribal relations.
BE PATIENT. Take time to understand, communicate with, and work with the Tribes who have an interest in NRC activities.
Identify Tribes that are not Federally recognized but have an interest in NRC regulatory activities using the specified Web sites (500nations.com, accessgenealogy.com, manataka.org) or SHPOs.
Contact the identified Tribes in the same manner as any member of the public.
